request.question: What is the second project of the vocalist of the band The Distillers?

title: FOCSA Building
Dol Theeta
Accidents Never Happen
The Distillers
Hristo Botev Stadium (Vratsa)
The ROADEX Project
VentureCrowd
Brody Dalle
Virginia HOT lanes
Catherine Hickson
sentences: In an era when concrete construction of buildings more than 18 storeys tall was considered unaffordable, the FOCSA was only the second project of its kind in the world.
The project was managed by engineer, Luis Sáenz Duplace; the architect was Ernesto Gómez Sampera (1921–2004), who later moved to Puerto Rico where he worked until his death in 2004.
Dol Theeta is a progressive metal band formed in 2008 by Greek music composer Thanasis Lightbridge.
The band represents the second project of Lightbridge's "Electronica Art Metal" vision, the first project being Dol Ammad a more powerful and epic progressive project.
Dol Theeta has a more atmospheric and psychedelic character and combines diverse musical styles such as heavy metal, electronica and opera.
Accidents Never Happen (ANH) was a rock band from Oslo, Norway, formed in 2005.

After releasing their debut album "Oslo Beat" in 2006, vocalist and main songwriter Magne Mostue, guitarist Martin Natvig, drummer Christian Løvhaug and guitarist Jesper Jørgensen moved to Liverpool, UK.
The band's music was influenced significantly by their two-year stay in the UK.

Their second project, "Accidents Never Happen", was recorded in The Motor Museum and St Brides church; (The band's rehearsal space) with Simon Cullwick (who allegedly turned down an offer to work on Razorlight's "Slipway Fires").
"Accidents Never Happen" was a much darker album than "Oslo Beat".
The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.
Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.
After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.
Hristo Botev Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Vratsa, Bulgaria.
It is currently used mostly for football matches.
It has been the home of Botev Vratsa for 60 years.
The stadium was built in 1948 and has a seating capacity of 8,000 spectators.
After the approval of a project worth € 150 000, in 2008 the stadium was renovated and had 3,000 seats, spread in the 3 central sectors.
In June 2009, the Botev Vratsa management approved a second project, worth € 120 000 for reconstruction of sector "V", opposite the central building of the stadium.
The second renovation was completed in 2009.

The ROADEX Project is a technical co-operation between roads organisations across northern Europe part funded by the EU Northern Periphery Programme that aims to share roads related information and research between the partners.

The Project was started in 1998 as a 3 year pilot co-operation between the roads districts of Finnish Lapland, Troms County of Norway, the Northern Region of Sweden and the Highland Council of Scotland and was subsequently followed and extended by a second project, ROADEX II, from 2002 to 2005, a third project, ROADEX III, from 2006 to 2007, and a final project, “ROADEX Network Implementing Accessibility”, from 2009 to 2012.
VentureCrowd is an Australian multi asset class crowdfunding platform, headquartered in Sydney, including equity crowdfunding, property crowdfunding and debt-based crowdfunding.
VentureCrowd completed the largest Australian equity crowdfunding raise, $4.2 million, for taxi-booking and payment software company Ingogo in May 2015.
This deal is ranked 8th on the List of highest funded equity crowdfunding projects.
This deal is ranked 8th on the List of highest funded equity crowdfunding projects.
In June 2016, VentureCrowd raised more than $900,000 for a Western Sydney residential project - a 35-lot development in Riverstone East, in partnership with the property developer ClearState.
In August 2016, a second project raised $1,700,000 for a 44-lot development project in Austral.
Brody Dalle (born Bree Joanna Alice Robinson; 1 January 1979) is an Australian-born singer-songwriter and guitarist.
Dalle began playing music in her adolescence, and moved to Los Angeles, California at age eighteen, where she found the punk rock band The Distillers.
The group released three albums before disbanding in 2006, and Dalle began another project, Spinnerette, releasing an eponymous album in 2009.
In 2014, she released "Diploid Love", her first album under her solo name.
Virginia HO/T lanes refers to five separate projects in the U.S. state of Virginia.
The first project, completed in November 2012, has added high-occupancy/toll (HO/T) lanes to the Capital Beltway (I-495) in Fairfax County.

The second project, opened to the public in December 2014, involved converting and extending the existing reversible high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes on I-95 and I-395 to HO/T lanes from Stafford to near Alexandria.
The third project, currently under construction (as of December 2016), will convert the existing high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes inside the beltway on Interstate 66 to HO/T lanes.
The fourth project will convert the existing high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lane outside the beltway on Interstate 66 and add a second lane to HO/T lanes.
The fifth project will convert the existing reversible high-occupancy vehicle (HOV) lanes on I-395 to HO/T lanes from near Alexandria to Washington, DC.
The sixth project will convert the existing reversible HOV Lanes to HOT Lanes on Interstate 64 from Interstate 564 in Norfolk to Interstate 264 in Virginia Beach.
Catherine Hickson (born in 1955) is a Canadian volcanologist formerly part of the Geological Survey of Canada.
Hickson has mapped and studied young volcanoes in Canada assessing their past and future eruptions.
In 2003, Hickson was one of the three scientists to report on a hypothetical eruption at the Canadian volcano Mount Cayley.
Hickson works in South America where some of the largest and highest volcanoes are, including those in Argentina, Bolivia, Chile and Peru.
She was the creator and leader of the Multinational Andean Project.
The project ran from 1996 - 2002, with Argentina, Bolivia, Chile, and Peru, being co-funded by the Canadian International Development Agency and the participating countries.
In 2002 a second project was started that focused on volcanoes, earthquake and landslides and was expanded to include Ecuador, Colombia, and Venezuela.
It ended in 2006.

The work garnered Hickson the prestigious C.J. Westerman Memorial Award (2010) presented by the Association of Professional Engineers and Geoscientists of BC, and the Head of the Public Service Award (1999) among other awards.

In addition to her scientific contributions, she has made contributions to many publications for young people and the public, as well as being a sought after lecturer, field trip leader and supporter of science for the public.
She was an eyewitness to the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens and wrote a book in 2005 describing her experience and explaining the mechanism of the eruption for the public.
She now works in the private sector.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What is the second project of the vocalist of the band The Distillers?
hop1_docs: The Distillers | The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.  Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.  After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.

Spinnerette | Spinnerette is an alternative rock band formed in 2007.  The band consists of Brody Dalle (the Distillers), Tony Bevilacqua (the Distillers), Jack Irons (What Is This? , Red Hot Chili Peppers, Walk the Moon, Eleven, Pearl Jam) and Alain Johannes (What Is This? , Walk the Moon, Eleven, Queens of the Stone Age, Them Crooked Vultures).  The band has been inactive since 2010 and its future is uncertain.

What Do I Do Now? | "What Do I Do Now?"  is a song by English Britpop band Sleeper, written by the band's vocalist and guitarist Louise Wener.  It was the first single to be released from their second album "The It Girl" in 1995 (see 1995 in British music).

Sound of Guns | Sound of Guns are an alternative rock band from Liverpool, England formed in 2008.  Discovered by "BBC Introducing" the band went on to sign with independent record label Distiller Records through which they released their debut album "What Came From Fire" on 28 June 2010 and second album "Angels and Enemies" on 5 March 2012.

Come What(ever) May | Come What(ever) May is the second studio album by American heavy metal band Stone Sour.  It was recorded and produced by the band and Nick Raskulinecz at Studio 606 in Los Angeles, California, and was released on August 1, 2006, by Roadrunner Records.  Writing for the album began as early as 2003 when vocalist Corey Taylor and guitarist James Root were writing material for their other band, Slipknot.  In January 2006, Stone Sour began recording their second album, during which time drummer Joel Ekman left the band because of family constraints.  He was eventually replaced by ex-Soulfly drummer Roy Mayorga who played on all but two tracks on the album.

Tony Martin (British singer) | Anthony Philip Harford (born 19 April 1957), better known by his stage name Tony Martin, is a heavy metal vocalist, best known for his time fronting Black Sabbath, initially from 1987 to 1991 and again from 1993 to 1997.  Martin was the band's second longest serving vocalist after Ozzy Osbourne.  He has since been involved in many other projects (such as M3, Misha Calvin, The Cage, Giuntini Project, and Phenomena).

That's What Friends Are For (Slade song) | That's What Friends Are For is a song by the British rock band Slade, released in 1987 as the second single from their fourteenth studio album "You Boyz Make Big Noize".  The song was written by lead vocalist Noddy Holder and bassist Jim Lea, and produced by Roy Thomas Baker.  It reached No. 95 in the UK, remaining in the charts for the one week.
hop1_titles: That's What Friends Are For (Slade song)
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage states Brody Dalle "went on to form Spinnerette" after The Distillers. Bridge entity: Brody Dalle. Missing fact: the name and chronological order of Brody Dalle's second musical project (which project is her second) is not given in these passages. Retrieval directions: 1) open Brody Dalle's full biography (e.g., "Brody Dalle" on Wikipedia) to find a chronological list of her bands/projects and their dates; 2) if the biography is unclear, open the "Spinnerette" page and Brody Dalle discography pages to confirm the sequence and identify the second project.
response.hop2_query: Open "Brody Dalle" (Wikipedia) to find a chronological list of her bands/projects and dates. If needed, open "Spinnerette" (Wikipedia) and Brody Dalle discography pages to confirm that Spinnerette was the project she formed after The Distillers. Provide the name of the second project and supporting dates.
hop2_docs: Brody Dalle | Brody Dalle (born Bree Joanna Alice Robinson; 1 January 1979) is an Australian-born singer-songwriter and guitarist.  Dalle began playing music in her adolescence, and moved to Los Angeles, California at age eighteen, where she found the punk rock band The Distillers.  The group released three albums before disbanding in 2006, and Dalle began another project, Spinnerette, releasing an eponymous album in 2009.  In 2014, she released "Diploid Love", her first album under her solo name.

The Distillers | The Distillers were an Australian-American punk rock band, formed in Los Angeles, California in 1998 by vocalist and guitarist Brody Dalle.  Dalle co-wrote, played guitar and provided vocals for nearly every track on the band's three albums.  After the breakup of the band in 2006, Dalle and Distillers guitarist Tony Bevilacqua went on to form Spinnerette.

Spinnerette | Spinnerette is an alternative rock band formed in 2007.  The band consists of Brody Dalle (the Distillers), Tony Bevilacqua (the Distillers), Jack Irons (What Is This? , Red Hot Chili Peppers, Walk the Moon, Eleven, Pearl Jam) and Alain Johannes (What Is This? , Walk the Moon, Eleven, Queens of the Stone Age, Them Crooked Vultures).  The band has been inactive since 2010 and its future is uncertain.

Diploid Love | Diploid Love is the debut solo album by Brody Dalle, former frontwoman of The Distillers and Spinnerette.  The digital download was released on April 28, 2014 in the United States and April 29 in Canada, followed by the CD and vinyl record set on May 19.  The lead single, "Meet the Foetus/Oh the Joy", was streamed for free on Soundcloud on February 9 before being released to iTunes on February 17.

David Hidalgo Jr. | David Hidalgo Jr. (born August 30, 1984) is an American drummer currently playing in Social Distortion.  Hidalgo replaced former drummer Scott Reeder, who had been busy with his main project Fu Manchu.  Prior to joining Social Distortion, he played drums in The Drips and Suicidal Tendencies.  He also plays drums for The Bronx/Mariachi El Bronx and the hardcore punk band Bullet Treatment with a variety of lineups that also included members of Rise Against, The Bronx, Cancer Bats, Anti-Flag and others.  David also toured with Brody Dalle in her Spinnerette and self-titled projects.  He has recorded and toured with Chuck Ragan and Dave Hause.  Most recently, Dave recorded on Greg Graffin's new solo record.  He is the son of David Hidalgo, guitarist and singer of Los Lobos.

Girls Talk (Garbage song) | "Girls Talk" is a stand-alone single released by alternative rock band Garbage for Record Store Day 2014.  Developed from a long gestating song idea, written by the band for inclusion on their 2007 greatest hits set "Absolute Garbage", "Girls Talk" features Brody Dalle (ex-The Distillers/Spinnerette).  Butch Vig described the original sessions for the song (then known as "Girls Talk Shit") as "pretty cool sounding, lots of fast pizzicato guitars and cellos".  The newly recorded take of "Girls Talk" features Eric Avery on bass guitar and was engineered and mixed by Billy Bush.

Afripedia Project | The Afripedia project was launched in mid-June 2012 and is ongoing.  It aims to expand offline Wikipedia access in French-speaking Africa, and encourage Africans to contribute to Wikipedia.  The project installs local Kiwix-serve wireless and intranet servers and provides training and maintenance support.
response.summary_2: Spinnerette is the vocalist Brody Dalle's second project after The Distillers. Sources state The Distillers broke up in 2006 and Dalle (with Tony Bevilacqua) formed Spinnerette (formed c.2007, self-titled album released 2009), before she later released her solo album Diploid Love in 2014.
